title: Early RTL delay prediction using neural networks
abstract: Nowadays, the digital chip design flow starts with formal specifications, which are mapped to Register Transfer Level (RTL) models using different underlying (micro-) architectures. By doing so, a hardware designer predicts and resolves time-critical parts to achieve an RTL-design that meets all constraints after synthesis. However, wrong predictions can be detected only later in the design flow, thus leading to long design iterations. Classical methods estimating delay in early design stages are constrained to the type of components or are computationally expensive for larger designs. This paper proposes a machine learningbased approach to estimate pin-to-pin delays for RTL combinational circuits. To improve the quality of the predictions we combine slew and delay estimation. To that end, training data are built using features of components generated by a model-driven hardware generator framework. Ground truth labels for delays, slews, and their interdependencies are extracted using open-source tools for logic synthesis and static timing analysis. Two different datasets are built: one targeting logic gates and multiplexers and an enlarged one, which generalizes to more RTL primitives A model trained using the former dataset achieves, on average, a coefficient of determination R2 of 87% when evaluating over 4-bit prefix adders. Using the enlarged dataset, the best model reaches an R2 of 77%. On average, our models are 8.4× faster w.r.t the time required to run synthesis and timing analysis. Results show that generalizing to more primitives decreases the models’ performance, but the runtime benefit is maintained. Based on the delay estimation, critical areas of the design can be detected and proper microarchitecture decisions can be taken earlier and faster in the design flow.
figure_captions: Fig. 1. General flow for dataset collection.

Fig. 1 sketches the whole flow for dataset generation. Our proposed method is independent of any RTL implementation. However, we use a HW generator framework, which generates our designs in a faster and automated way by using meta-modeling. The used HW generation framework is MetaRTL [27]. It applies the Model Driven Architecture (MDA) principle to RTL generation [28]. The abstract model (Computation Independent Model (CIM) in MDA) hierarchically describes configuration options.

Fig. 4 shows the MI (gray bars) and F-test statistic (orange bars) between the selected features. Fig. 4(a) shows the highest degree of linear dependency measured by the F-test is between the output slew and the features input slew X7 and fanout X6, while the MI is the highest for features related to input bit widths X9 and X10. Similarly, the MI between delay and X9 and X10 are the most significant, as shown in Fig. 4(b). Moreover, the F-test indicates that the most relevant linear dependency occurs between the delay and the output bit width X4.

Fig. 3 shows the SRCC matrix pairwise calculated for both datasets. For DS-I, all the features Xicorrelate with the objectives Y2. As expected, there is a high correlation between Y2 and X7 features and with structure-related features such as X1, X2, and X3. Similarly, the features Xicorrelate with the objectives Y2 for DS-II. As expected, there is a high correlation between Y1 and X6 features. The lower correlation between X5 with other features and objectives is because only one component type of the dataset has multiple outputs. For the remaining component types, the number of outputs is fixed to one.

Fig. 4. MI (bars colored in gray) and F-Test (bars colored in orange) values for falling edge in DS-II. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fig. 5. Shapley values for falling edge in DS-II.
Fig. 6. Avg. results of estimation in test set for DS-I and DS-II.
Fig. 7. From left to right: BKA, KSA, SKA. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fig. 8. Runtime comparison between ML models and EDA tools.

Fig. 8 shows the average inference time and the time required to synthesize and run the STA tool for the same designs. On average, any ML model is 8.4× faster. For the 64-bit adders, the time benefit is more evident, as the ML models are faster by 11.5×.

Table 1 lists our feature set Xand objectives Y. In this subsection, the motivation of our selection is presented:
Table 6 R2 of both models compared with STA tool results.
Table 5 Implementation details for the three adders.

Table 6 compares our estimated delay values w.r.t the results from the STA tool, when using the MLP and the TabMLP architectures. Table 6 shows the R2 value for fall and rise delay estimation. The MLP without slew information provides a low estimation accuracy. To increase the accuracy, we add slew features to the dataset. We incorporate slew estimation and propagation in our final method, as the input slew is known only for the input ports. The second last row of Table 6 confirms the strategy and shows the high R2 values. The same experiments were conducted using TabMLP.
Table 7 Evaluation of models trained with DS-I for adders with different input slews variants.

Table 7 shows the average results for both models when trained over DS-I. The averaged R2 coefficient for MLP is 0.87 and for TabMLP, 0.79. Thus, the averaged absolute difference for the R2 coefficient between both models is 0.08. In this case, the best architecture is MLP. When training with DS-II, the averaged R2 coefficients shown in Table 8 were 0.67 and 0.77 for MLP and TabMLP, respectively. Thus, TabMLP outperforms MLP by 0.1.
Table 8 Evaluation of models trained with DS-II for adders with different input slews variants.
